title: Encode and Decode Strings
constraints: `strs[i]` contains any possible characters out of `256` valid ASCII characters.

hint_plan.level_1: Break down the core operation in Encode and Decode Strings.
hint_plan.level_2: Look for the data structure or traversal pattern that avoids repeated work.
hint_plan.level_3: Use the constraints and examples to narrow the intended time complexity.
hint_plan.level_4: Implement the direct approach that maintains the required invariant step by step.

canonical_solution_summary: Implement the direct approach that maintains the required invariant step by step.
hints_per_sentence: Start by defining the class named Codec as given in the starter code.
Define the encode method that takes a list of strings as input.
Check if the input list is empty to handle the edge case.
If it's empty, return an empty string as the encoded result.
Initialize an empty list to build the encoded string parts.
Loop through each string in the input list.
For each string, append its length, a delimiter like '#', and the string itself to the list.
Join all parts into a single encoded string and return it.
Define the decode method that takes the encoded string as input.
Check if the encoded string is empty to handle the edge case.
Initialize an empty list to store the decoded strings.
Set an index variable to start parsing from the beginning of the encoded string.
Continue parsing until you reach the end of the encoded string.
Find the position of the delimiter '#' starting from the current index.
Extract the length of the next string by converting the substring before '#' to an integer.
Move the index past the delimiter to the start of the actual string.
Append the substring of the specified length to the result list.
Update the index to move past the current string for the next iteration.
Return the list of decoded strings.
final_explanation: The solution uses a length-prefix encoding scheme where each string is prefixed with its length and a delimiter (like '#') to separate it from the actual content. This ensures that during decoding, you can accurately parse each string by reading the length first, then extracting exactly that many characters, avoiding ambiguity even if strings contain special characters. This approach efficiently handles empty lists and strings while maintaining the original order.
